critique.summary: The article describes 'Get Shit Done' (GSD), a lightweight meta-prompting, context engineering, and spec-driven development system designed to improve the reliability and quality of AI-generated code using Claude Code and other AI runtimes. It outlines the system's workflow, features, commands, and benefits for solo developers seeking an efficient, low-overhead development process without enterprise complexity.
mainPoints: GSD addresses context rot in AI code generation by managing context windows effectively.
It provides a structured workflow including project initialization, phase discussion, planning, execution, verification, and milestone management.
The system uses multi-agent orchestration, atomic git commits per task, and XML prompt formatting for precise instructions and verification.
GSD supports multiple AI runtimes such as Claude Code, OpenCode, Gemini CLI, Codex, Copilot, and Antigravity.
It is designed for solo developers or small teams who want to avoid complex enterprise processes while maintaining high code quality.
The system includes commands for managing phases, sessions, UI design, and troubleshooting, with options for granular permissions and security measures.
GSD is open source, MIT licensed, and has a large user base with thousands of stars and forks on GitHub.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about GSD's features, workflow, and supported runtimes are consistent with the publicly available GitHub repository and documentation. The description of the system's design principles and commands matches the repository's README and usage instructions. The user testimonials and adoption claims by engineers at major companies are stated but not independently verified within the article. Overall, the technical details and functionality described are accurate based on the source repository, but some promotional claims lack external corroboration.
considerations: The article does not provide independent third-party reviews or comparisons with other similar tools.
User testimonials and claims of adoption by engineers at major companies are unverified and anecdotal.
Potential limitations, challenges, or drawbacks of using GSD are not discussed.
Security recommendations are mentioned but the effectiveness of built-in protections is not independently evaluated.
The article assumes familiarity with AI code generation tools and may not address the learning curve for new users.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around user experiences with the Get Shit Done (GSD) tool and comparisons with other frameworks. Users share their opinions on the effectiveness and efficiency of GSD in coding workflows.

comments.author: gbrindisi
comments.text:
I like openspec, it lets you tune the workflow to your liking and doesn’t get in the way.

I started with all the standard spec flow and as I got more confident and opinionated I simplified it to my liking.

I think the point of any spec driven framework is that you want to eventually own the workflow yourself, so that you can constraint code generation on your own terms.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a constructive perspective on the flexibility of openspec, indicating a positive user experience and understanding of workflow customization.

comments.author: maccam912
comments.text: I've had a good experience with https://github.com/obra/superpowers. At first glance this looks similar. Has anyone tried both who can offer a comparison?
comments.analysis: The request for comparison demonstrates a desire for informed discussion, although it lacks personal insight or experience.

comments.author: MeetingsBrowser
comments.text:
I've tried it, and I'm not convinced I got measurably better results than just prompting claude code directly.

It absolutely tore through tokens though. I don't normally hit my session limits, but hit the 5-hour limits in ~30 minutes and my weekly limits by Tuesday with GSD.
comments.analysis: While the comment reflects skepticism about GSD's effectiveness, it could be seen as subjective and lacking broader context or evidence.

comments.author: dfltr
comments.text: GSD has a reputation for being a token burner compared to something like Superpowers. Has that changed lately? Always open to revisiting things as they improve.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a critical view of GSD's efficiency, referencing its reputation, but lacks personal experience or recent updates.

comments.author: prakashrj
comments.text: With GSD, I was able to write 250K lines of code in less than a month, without prior knowledge of claude.
comments.analysis: While the claim is impressive, it lacks supporting details and could be seen as exaggerated without further context.

comments.author: greenchair
comments.analysis: This comment is dismissive and does not contribute constructively to the discussion, focusing solely on a negative opinion.

comments.author: obsidianbases1
comments.text:
> If you know clearly what you want

This is the real challenge. The people I know that jump around to new tools have a tough time explaining what they want, and thus how new tool is better than last tool.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights a common issue in tool adoption but lacks specific examples or actionable insights.
